    Introduction to pandora-sat¶

    pandora-sat is a simulator for Pandora data that is currently under development.

    In [1]:
    Copied! 
    %load_ext autoreload 
%autoreload 2
    %load_ext autoreload %autoreload 2
    In [2]:
    Copied! 
    import pandorasat as ps
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import astropy.units as u
plt.style.use('seaborn-white')
    import pandorasat as ps import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import numpy as np import astropy.units as u plt.style.use('seaborn-white')
    /tmp/ipykernel_2928/4230182660.py:5: MatplotlibDeprecationWarning: The seaborn styles shipped by Matplotlib are deprecated since 3.6, as they no longer correspond to the styles shipped by seaborn. However, they will remain available as 'seaborn-v0_8-<style>'. Alternatively, directly use the seaborn API instead.
  plt.style.use('seaborn-white')

    You can initialize the observatory using

    In [3]:
    Copied! 
    p = ps.PandoraSat()
    p = ps.PandoraSat()

    You can see different detectors of the observatory using

    In [4]:
    Copied! 
    nirda = p.NIRDA
visda = p.VISDA
    nirda = p.NIRDA visda = p.VISDA

    Basic Detector Properties¶

    In [5]:
    Copied! 
    w = np.arange(0.1, 2, 0.01) * u.micron
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(w, nirda.sensitivity(w), label='NIRDA', c='k', ls='-')
ax.plot(w, visda.sensitivity(w), label='VISDA', c='k', ls='--')
ax.set(xlabel='Wavelength [microns]', ylabel=f"Sensitivity [{nirda.sensitivity(w).unit.to_string('latex')}]")
plt.legend()
    w = np.arange(0.1, 2, 0.01) * u.micron fig, ax = plt.subplots() ax.plot(w, nirda.sensitivity(w), label='NIRDA', c='k', ls='-') ax.plot(w, visda.sensitivity(w), label='VISDA', c='k', ls='--') ax.set(xlabel='Wavelength [microns]', ylabel=f"Sensitivity [{nirda.sensitivity(w).unit.to_string('latex')}]") plt.legend()
    Out[5]:
    <matplotlib.legend.Legend at 0x7fe6fbb3c760>

    You can estimate the flux/magnitude of a source

    In [6]:
    Copied! 
    nirda.flux_from_mag(10)
    nirda.flux_from_mag(10)
    Out[6]:
    $3.6386925 \times 10^{-14} \; \mathrm{\frac{erg}{\mathring{A}\,s\,cm^{2}}}$
    In [7]:
    Copied! 
    visda.flux_from_mag(10)
    visda.flux_from_mag(10)
    Out[7]:
    $3.7603683 \times 10^{-13} \; \mathrm{\frac{erg}{\mathring{A}\,s\,cm^{2}}}$
    In [8]:
    Copied! 
    nirda.mag_from_flux(1e-15 * u.erg/(u.AA * u.s * u.cm**2))
    nirda.mag_from_flux(1e-15 * u.erg/(u.AA * u.s * u.cm**2))
    Out[8]:
    $13.902363 \; \mathrm{}$
    In [9]:
    Copied! 
    visda.mag_from_flux(1e-15 * u.erg/(u.AA * u.s * u.cm**2))
    visda.mag_from_flux(1e-15 * u.erg/(u.AA * u.s * u.cm**2))
    Out[9]:
    $16.438076 \; \mathrm{}$

    Detector have properties

    In [10]:
    Copied! 
    visda.dark
    visda.dark
    Out[10]:
    $1 \; \mathrm{\frac{e^{-}}{s}}$
    In [11]:
    Copied! 
    visda.read_noise
    visda.read_noise
    Out[11]:
    $1.5 \; \mathrm{e^{-}}$

    CH: ADD NIRDA NOISE PROPERTIES¶

    You can take a look at the PSFs and PRFs for each detector with the diagnose function

    In [12]:
    Copied! 
    nirda.diagnose(n=3, image_type='psf');
nirda.diagnose(n=3, image_type='prf');
    nirda.diagnose(n=3, image_type='psf'); nirda.diagnose(n=3, image_type='prf');
    In [13]:
    Copied! 
    visda.diagnose(n=3, image_type='psf');
visda.diagnose(n=3, image_type='prf');
    visda.diagnose(n=3, image_type='psf'); visda.diagnose(n=3, image_type='prf');

    Targets¶

    To make simulations you'll need targets with information about the SED. You can get those either using:

    In [14]:
    Copied! 
    target = ps.Target('GJ 436').from_vizier()
    target = ps.Target('GJ 436').from_vizier()
    In [15]:
    Copied! 
    target.plot_spectrum();
    target.plot_spectrum();

    or

    In [16]:
    Copied! 
    target = ps.Target('GJ 436').from_phoenix()
    target = ps.Target('GJ 436').from_phoenix()
    In [17]:
    Copied! 
    target.plot_spectrum();
    target.plot_spectrum();

    targets have some helpful attributes including

    In [18]:
    Copied! 
    target.ra
    target.ra
    Out[18]:
    175.54622244662
    In [19]:
    Copied! 
    target.dec
    target.dec
    Out[19]:
    26.706571692
    In [20]:
    Copied! 
    target.teff
    target.teff
    Out[20]:
    3456.0
    In [21]:
    Copied! 
    target.jmag
    target.jmag
    Out[21]:
    6.900000095367432
    In [22]:
    Copied! 
    target.tmag
    target.tmag
    Out[22]:
    8.402999877929688

    Making Visible Image Simulations¶

    To make image simulations you need to point the observatory at a target and get the sky catalog

    In [23]:
    Copied! 
    target = ps.Target('HAT-P-19').from_phoenix()
    target = ps.Target('HAT-P-19').from_phoenix()
    In [24]:
    Copied! 
    target.planets['b']['pl_orbper']
    target.planets['b']['pl_orbper']
    Out[24]:
    $4.008778 \; \mathrm{d}$
    In [25]:
    Copied! 
    # the observatory has the `get_sky_catalog` method for now
source_catalog = p.get_sky_catalog(target.ra, target.dec)
    # the observatory has the `get_sky_catalog` method for now source_catalog = p.get_sky_catalog(target.ra, target.dec)
    In [26]:
    Copied! 
    source_catalog.head()
    source_catalog.head()
    Out[26]:
    ra dec mag vis_x vis_y nir_x nir_y vis_counts vis_flux
    0 9.516723 34.711542 13.0889 1024.000000 1024.000000 1064.000000 2048.000000 7534.490929 2.186102e-14
    1 9.539047 34.693664 13.0567 939.285261 941.493870 1008.472692 1993.920352 7761.290057 2.251907e-14
    2 9.507223 34.750926 14.1134 1060.025604 1205.774581 1087.613421 2167.146364 2932.607320 8.508840e-15
    3 9.498315 34.657354 15.2956 1093.886844 773.906170 1109.808184 1884.072952 987.129008 2.864115e-15
    4 9.506904 34.770621 14.2464 1061.226987 1296.671866 1088.400883 2226.726097 2594.501430 7.527840e-15
    In [27]:
    Copied! 
    len(source_catalog)
    len(source_catalog)
    Out[27]:
    102

    You can then get an image of the sky on the visible camera

    In [28]:
    Copied! 
    # The observatory get's the sky image because eventually it will return images from both detectors.
nreads = 4
img = p.get_sky_image(source_catalog, nreads=nreads, include_noise=True)
    # The observatory get's the sky image because eventually it will return images from both detectors. nreads = 4 img = p.get_sky_image(source_catalog, nreads=nreads, include_noise=True)
    ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

    Below is a plot of the VISDA sky image. In future, this will also great a NIRDA image.

    In [32]:
    Copied! 
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
plt.imshow(img.value, origin='lower', vmin=100, vmax=150, cmap='Greys_r')
cbar = plt.colorbar()
cbar.set_label('Counts [e$^-$]', fontsize=12)
plt.title(f'Visible Channel, {target}, {visda.integration_time*nreads} Integration', fontsize=15)
plt.xlabel("X Pixel", fontsize=13)
plt.ylabel("Y Pixel", fontsize=13)
k = np.hypot(source_catalog.vis_x - visda.naxis1.value/2, source_catalog.vis_y - visda.naxis2.value/2) < 1320/2
plt.scatter(source_catalog.vis_x[k], source_catalog.vis_y[k], edgecolor='r', facecolor='None', s=100, label='Background Stars')
plt.scatter(source_catalog.vis_x[0], source_catalog.vis_y[0], edgecolor='lime', facecolor='None', s=100, label=f'{target}')
plt.legend(frameon=True)
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8)) plt.imshow(img.value, origin='lower', vmin=100, vmax=150, cmap='Greys_r') cbar = plt.colorbar() cbar.set_label('Counts [e$^-$]', fontsize=12) plt.title(f'Visible Channel, {target}, {visda.integration_time*nreads} Integration', fontsize=15) plt.xlabel("X Pixel", fontsize=13) plt.ylabel("Y Pixel", fontsize=13) k = np.hypot(source_catalog.vis_x - visda.naxis1.value/2, source_catalog.vis_y - visda.naxis2.value/2) < 1320/2 plt.scatter(source_catalog.vis_x[k], source_catalog.vis_y[k], edgecolor='r', facecolor='None', s=100, label='Background Stars') plt.scatter(source_catalog.vis_x[0], source_catalog.vis_y[0], edgecolor='lime', facecolor='None', s=100, label=f'{target}') plt.legend(frameon=True)

    Making NIRDA Traces¶

    You can get an example trace of a target on the NIRDA detector using a

    In [33]:
    Copied! 
    wavelength = np.linspace(0.1, 2, 6000) * u.micron
spectrum = target.model_spectrum(wavelength)
trace = nirda.get_trace(wavelength, spectrum, pixel_resolution=2)
    wavelength = np.linspace(0.1, 2, 6000) * u.micron spectrum = target.model_spectrum(wavelength) trace = nirda.get_trace(wavelength, spectrum, pixel_resolution=2)
    In [34]:
    Copied! 
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 3))
plt.pcolormesh(np.arange(trace.shape[1]), np.arange(trace.shape[0]), trace.value)
cbar = plt.colorbar()
cbar.set_label("Electrons per Second")
plt.title('GJ 436', fontsize=20)
ax.set(xlabel='Pixels', ylabel='Pixels')
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 3)) plt.pcolormesh(np.arange(trace.shape[1]), np.arange(trace.shape[0]), trace.value) cbar = plt.colorbar() cbar.set_label("Electrons per Second") plt.title('GJ 436', fontsize=20) ax.set(xlabel='Pixels', ylabel='Pixels')
    Out[34]:
    [Text(0.5, 0, 'Pixels'), Text(0, 0.5, 'Pixels')]

    Making realistic data simulations¶

    In [35]:
    Copied! 
    target = ps.Target('HAT-P-19').from_phoenix()
    target = ps.Target('HAT-P-19').from_phoenix()

    Find the time baseline for observations

    In [36]:
    Copied! 
    # Set a number of frames
nframes = 40
exptime = nframes * nirda.frame_time
time = np.arange(target.planets['b']['pl_tranmid'].value - 0.3, target.planets['b']['pl_tranmid'].value + 0.3, exptime.to(u.day).value)

# Drop some parts of the orbit because of Earth occultation
c = (90*u.minute).to(u.day).value
time = time[((time % c) > c * 0.3)]
cyc = time - (time % c)
time = time[(cyc != np.unique(cyc)[0]) & (cyc != np.unique(cyc)[-1])]
    # Set a number of frames nframes = 40 exptime = nframes * nirda.frame_time time = np.arange(target.planets['b']['pl_tranmid'].value - 0.3, target.planets['b']['pl_tranmid'].value + 0.3, exptime.to(u.day).value) # Drop some parts of the orbit because of Earth occultation c = (90*u.minute).to(u.day).value time = time[((time % c) > c * 0.3)] cyc = time - (time % c) time = time[(cyc != np.unique(cyc)[0]) & (cyc != np.unique(cyc)[-1])]
    In [37]:
    Copied! 
    plt.scatter(time, target.box_transit(time))
    plt.scatter(time, target.box_transit(time))
    Out[37]:
    <matplotlib.collections.PathCollection at 0x7fe6f875e9a0>

    integration = nirda.get_integration(wavelength, spectrum, nframes=nframes)

    In [42]:
    Copied! 
    %lprun -f nirda.psf.prf nirda.get_trace(wavelength, spectrum.value**0 * spectrum.unit, target_center=[40, 250])
    %lprun -f nirda.psf.prf nirda.get_trace(wavelength, spectrum.value**0 * spectrum.unit, target_center=[40, 250])
    In [43]:
    Copied! 
    nx, ny = 8, 8
xcs, ycs = np.linspace(-0.5, 0.5, nx + 1)[:-1], np.linspace(-0.5, 0.5, ny + 1)[:-1]
psfs = np.zeros((nx, ny, 80, 50))
for idx, xc in tqdm(enumerate(xcs), position=0, leave=True, total=nx):
    for jdx, yc in enumerate(ycs):
        x1, y1, f = nirda.psf.prf(nirda.psf.midpoint, location=[40+yc, 25+xc])
        X, Y = np.asarray(np.meshgrid(x1, y1)).astype(int)
        psfs[idx, jdx, X, Y] = f.T
    nx, ny = 8, 8 xcs, ycs = np.linspace(-0.5, 0.5, nx + 1)[:-1], np.linspace(-0.5, 0.5, ny + 1)[:-1] psfs = np.zeros((nx, ny, 80, 50)) for idx, xc in tqdm(enumerate(xcs), position=0, leave=True, total=nx): for jdx, yc in enumerate(ycs): x1, y1, f = nirda.psf.prf(nirda.psf.midpoint, location=[40+yc, 25+xc]) X, Y = np.asarray(np.meshgrid(x1, y1)).astype(int) psfs[idx, jdx, X, Y] = f.T
